@@ -492,6 +494,7 @@
 {"id":"franken_networkx-l85","title":"Layout Algorithms: force-directed, bipartite, BFS, spiral layouts","description":"## Scope
Implement 8 graph layout functions:
- `arf_layout` — Attractive-Repulsive Force layout
- `bfs_layout` — BFS-based hierarchical layout
- `bipartite_layout` — position bipartite graph in two columns/rows
- `forceatlas2_layout` — ForceAtlas2 algorithm (popular in Gephi)
- `fruchterman_reingold_layout` — Fruchterman-Reingold force-directed (spring_layout)
- `multipartite_layout` — position multipartite graph in columns
- `rescale_layout_dict` — rescale positions to fit in a bounding box
- `spiral_layout` — position nodes along a spiral

## Implementation Approach
- Most can be implemented in Python with numpy for vector operations
- ForceAtlas2: iterative force simulation. Can optionally use Barnes-Hut approximation for O(V log V) per iteration.
- Fruchterman-Reingold: standard spring-electrical model. Already common, may delegate to NetworkX.
- bipartite_layout / multipartite_layout: simple coordinate assignment based on partition membership.
- BFS layout: compute BFS layers, assign x by layer, y by position in layer.
- spiral_layout: parametric curve, assign nodes along Archimedean spiral.
- arf_layout: similar to force-directed but with specific attractive-repulsive function.
- rescale_layout_dict: simple affine transformation.

## Implementation Location
- Python: `python/franken_networkx/drawing/layout.py`
- No Rust needed (layout is not performance-critical for typical graph sizes)

## Test Requirements
- Verify output is dict of {node: (x, y)} pairs
- Verify no overlapping positions for reasonable graphs
- Verify bipartite_layout places nodes in correct partitions
- Cross-validate against NetworkX layouts","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-21T19:13:12.197932948Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-22T03:18:43.081547908Z","closed_at":"2026-03-22T03:18:43.081005561Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["layout","tier-3"]}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-w7ge","title":"NX-REPLACE: Misc algorithms — euler, cycles, hashing, coloring, similarity, etc.","description":"## Functions to replace (22)
Euler/structural:
1. eulerize (__init__.py:6123) — add edges to make graph Eulerian
2. moral_graph (__init__.py:6133) — marry parents in DAG
3. minimum_cycle_basis (__init__.py:6160) — Horton's algorithm
4. chordless_cycles (__init__.py:6169) — enumerate chordless cycles

Graph hashing:
5. weisfeiler_lehman_graph_hash (__init__.py:6454) — WL hash for isomorphism
6. weisfeiler_lehman_subgraph_hashes (__init__.py:6463) — WL subgraph hashes

Coloring:
7. equitable_color (__init__.py:6800) — equitable graph coloring
8. chromatic_polynomial (__init__.py:6808) — deletion-contraction

Similarity/distance:
9. panther_similarity (__init__.py:7001) — random-walk similarity
10. panther_vector_similarity (__init__.py:7170) — vector variant
11. cd_index (__init__.py:7229) — consolidation-disruption index

Misc:
12. local_bridges (__init__.py:1345) — edges whose removal increases distance
13. complete_to_chordal_graph (__init__.py:6938) — chordal completion
14. effective_graph_resistance (__init__.py:7201) — Kirchhoff index
15. goldberg_radzik (__init__.py:7237) — shortest paths with negative weights
16. communicability_exp (__init__.py:7162) — matrix exponential
17. local_constraint (__init__.py:7123) — Burt's constraint measure
18. is_perfect_graph (__init__.py:7486) — perfect graph recognition
19. k_factor (__init__.py:7541) — k-regular spanning subgraph
20. spectral_graph_forge (__init__.py:7550) — spectral graph generation
21. mycielskian (__init__.py:7293) — Mycielski graph construction
22. random_powerlaw_tree_sequence (__init__.py:8299) — power-law degree sequence

## Priority tiers
- Tier 1 (moderate complexity, good value): eulerize, moral_graph, WL hashes, mycielskian
- Tier 2 (complex algorithms): minimum_cycle_basis, equitable_color, chromatic_polynomial
- Tier 3 (exotic, OK to keep as NX): spectral_graph_forge, panther_similarity, effective_graph_resistance

## Files to modify
- crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs: add Rust implementations
- crates/fnx-python/src/algorithms.rs: add bindings
- python/franken_networkx/__init__.py: rewire

## Acceptance criteria
- Each replaced function matches NX output
- Tier 3 functions may remain as NX delegation if complexity is prohibitive","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-03T00:20:16.777801946Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-03T02:42:08.015140569Z","closed_at":"2026-04-03T02:42:08.014895194Z","close_reason":"Superseded: split into 3 smaller beads (misc-tier1, misc-tier2, misc-tier3) for better parallelism and clearer scope","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-y1g","title":"Coloring & Planarity: equitable coloring, chromatic polynomial, embeddings","description":"## Scope
Implement 4 coloring and planarity functions:
- `equitable_color` — find equitable k-coloring (color classes differ by at most 1 in size)
- `chromatic_polynomial` — compute the chromatic polynomial of a graph
- `combinatorial_embedding_to_pos` — convert combinatorial planar embedding to coordinate positions
- `apply_matplotlib_colors` — utility to apply color scheme to matplotlib visualization

## Algorithmic Approach
- equitable_color: Use the Kierstead-Kostochka procedure. Start with a greedy coloring, then equalize class sizes by recoloring chains. O(V * k) where k is number of colors.
- chromatic_polynomial: Deletion-contraction recursion. P(G,k) = P(G-e,k) - P(G/e,k). NP-hard in general. Use memoization on graph isomorphism classes. For small graphs (<20 nodes) this is feasible. Can also use Whitney rank polynomial relationship.
- combinatorial_embedding_to_pos: Given face structure from planar embedding, use Tutte's barycentric method or Schnyder realizer to assign coordinates. O(V) for Tutte embedding.
- apply_matplotlib_colors: Simple utility, map color indices to matplotlib colormap values.

## Implementation Location
- Python: `python/franken_networkx/algorithms/coloring/` and `python/franken_networkx/algorithms/planarity.py`
- chromatic_polynomial could benefit from Rust for memoized recursion

## Edge Cases
- equitable_color: graph requiring more than Delta+1 colors (should raise)
- chromatic_polynomial: very slow for dense graphs > 15 nodes
- combinatorial_embedding_to_pos: non-planar input (should raise or handle gracefully)

## Test Requirements
- equitable_color: verify valid coloring AND equitability (class sizes differ by <= 1)
- chromatic_polynomial: verify P(G,k) = 0 for k < chi(G), P(K_n, k) = k(k-1)...(k-n+1)
- Cross-validate against NetworkX","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-21T19:14:02.971518597Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-23T07:35:00.719565439Z","closed_at":"2026-03-23T07:35:00.719053861Z","close_reason":"Implemented 3 coloring/planarity: equitable_color, chromatic_polynomial, combinatorial_embedding_to_pos.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["algorithms","coloring","planarity","tier-3"]}
